content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • •• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

January 6, 1977

77-01-091

Mr. O. James Gibson, Manager
State and Local Government Relations
Public Affairs Division
Atlantic-Richfield Company
515 South Flower Street
Los Angeles, CA 90051

Re: Opinion Request No. 76-093

Dear Mr. Gibson:

Thank you for your letter of December 15, 1976,
seeking an opinion with respect to when certain expenditures
must be reported. Because your opinion request does not raise
a substantial question of interpretation under the Political
Reform Act, no formal opinion will be issued. I hope, however,
that the following informal advice will resolve your problem.

You ask whether Atlantic-Richfield Company can delay
reporting certain expenditures incurred prior to the closing
date of its last preelection report when the exact cost of
those expenditures will not be known until after the election.
You suggest that it may be appropriate to delay the disclosure
of the expenditures in question until the postelection campaign
statement.

Government Code Section 82025 provides that "An ex-
penditure is made on the date the payment is made or on the
date consideration, if any, is received, whichever is earlier."
Government Code Section 84210(h) requires that all expenditures
incurred "during the period covered by the campaign statement"
be disclosed on the campaign statement due at the end of the
period. In the case of the newspaper advertisements to which
you refer, the consideration is received, at a minimum, no later
than the time when those advertisements appear in the newspaper.
Accordingly, the expenditure for the newspaper advertisements
has been made during the period in which the advertisements ap-
pear and must be disclosed on the campaign statement which cov-
ers that period.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (DIA) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5001 • ENFORCEMENT-CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

Mr. O. James [illegible]son
Page Two
January 6, 1977


     If you cannot determine the exact cost of the expen-
diture, a reasonable estimate will suffice since Government
Code Section 81004 only requires "reasonable diligence" in the
preparation of a campaign statement.  After the exact amount
of the expenditure is known, you can then amend the campaign
statement to make it accurate.  This procedure best comports
with the purposes of the Political Reform Act by insuring dis-
closure of at least the recipient of the expenditure, although
not the exact amount, prior to the election.

     If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion re-
quest you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321.
If you have any further questions, please contact Kenneth Finney,
Chief of the Commission's Legal Division.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

MB:KF:jo

sections.question: You ask whether Atlantic-Richfield Company can delay reporting certain expenditures incurred prior to the closing date of its last preelection report when the exact cost of those expenditures will not be known until after the election. You suggest that it may be appropriate to delay the disclosure of the expenditures in question until the postelection campaign statement.
sections.conclusion: In the case of the newspaper advertisements to which you refer, the consideration is received, at a minimum, no later than the time when those advertisements appear in the newspaper. Accordingly, the expenditure for the newspaper advertisements has been made during the period in which the advertisements appear and must be disclosed on the campaign statement which covers that period. If you cannot determine the exact cost of the expenditure, a reasonable estimate will suffice since Government Code Section 81004 requires "reasonable diligence" in the preparation of a campaign statement. After the exact amount of the expenditure is known, you can then amend the campaign statement to make it accurate.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a corporation delay reporting expenditures incurred before a preelection report deadline when the exact cost is unknown until after the election, and can such disclosure be postponed to the postelection campaign statement?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Expenditures must be reported on the campaign statement covering the period when consideration is received (when advertisements appear). If the exact cost is unknown, a reasonable estimate satisfies the disclosure requirement, and the statement can be amended once the exact amount is determined.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an informal advice letter (not a formal opinion) in response to an opinion request deemed not to raise a substantial question. Document contains clear question and conclusion sections with supporting legal analysis.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You ask whether Atlantic-Richfield Company can delay reporting certain expenditures incurred prior to the closing date of its last preelection report when the exact cost of those expenditures will not be known until after the election. You suggest that it may be appropriate to delay the disclosure of the expenditures in question until the postelection campaign statement.

CONCLUSION: In the case of the newspaper advertisements to which you refer, the consideration is received, at a minimum, no later than the time when those advertisements appear in the newspaper. Accordingly, the expenditure for the newspaper advertisements has been made during the period in which the advertisements appear and must be disclosed on the campaign statement which covers that period. If you cannot determine the exact cost of the expenditure, a reasonable estimate will suffice since Government Code Section 81004 requires "reasonable diligence" in the preparation of a campaign statement. After the exact amount of the expenditure is known, you can then amend the campaign statement to make it accurate.

I hope, however, that the following informal advice will resolve your problem. You ask whether Atlantic-Richfield Company can delay reporting certain expenditures incurred prior to the closing date of its last preelection report when the exact cost of those expenditures will not be known until after the election. You suggest that it may be appropriate to delay the disclosure of the expenditures in question until the postelection campaign statement. Government Code Section 82025 provides that "An ex- penditure is made on the date the payment is made or on the date consideration, if any, is received, whichever is earlier." Government Code Section 84210(h) requires that all expenditures incurred "during the period covered by the campaign statement" be disclosed on the campaign statement due at the end of the period. TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (DIA) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5001 • ENFORCEMENT-CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441 Mr. O. James [illegible]son Page Two January 6, 1977 If you cannot determine the exact cost of the expen- diture, a reasonable estimate will suffice since Government Code Section 81004 only requires "reasonable diligence" in the preparation of a campaign statement. After the exact amount of the expenditure is known, you can then amend the campaign statement to make it accurate. embedding.summary: FPPC advises that Atlantic-Richfield Company must report newspaper advertisement expenditures on the preelection campaign statement during the period the advertisements appear, not delay disclosure to the postelection statement. Reasonable estimates are acceptable with amendment once exact amounts are known.
